rationale: The page is an infographic titled 'World: Rivers/Water Bodies in News' which provides detailed geographical, environmental, and infrastructural information about six major water bodies: Tulare Lake, Aral Sea, Congo River, Blue Nile, Great Ruaha River, and Seine River. This content is highly suitable for generating multiple-choice questions on geography and environmental science.
cleanedText:
7.2.2. RIVERS/WATER BODIES IN NEWS

World: Rivers/Water Bodies in News

Tulare Lake
- California's Tulare Lake, reemerged after 130 Years due to 2023's atmospheric rivers.
- Location: San Joaquin Valley, California
- Kern River, fed by snowmelt from the southern Sierra replenishes Tulare Lake.

Aral Sea
- Research indicates signs of geological changes since water disappeared.
- Location: Boundary between Kazakhstan (North) and Uzbekistan (South).
- Cause of Disappearance: Diversion of Amu and Syr Darya rivers.
- Dried-up Aral Sea became hazardous Aralkum Desert.

Congo River
- The World Bank approved $250 million in financing for the Inga 3 Hydropower project.
- River Basin covers 9 countries- Zambia, Angola, Democratic Republic of Congo, Republic of Congo, Central African Republic, Tanzania, Burundi, Rwanda, and Cameroon before emptying into the Atlantic Ocean.
- World's 2nd largest in terms of flow after Amazon, 2nd longest river in Africa after Nile, deepest river in the world and crosses equator twice.
- Congo basin has the world's largest tropical peatland, holding ~3 years of global carbon emissions from fossil fuels.

Blue Nile
- Construction of Ethiopia's Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam (GERD), Africa's Largest HEP completed.
- Major tributary of Nile.
- Flows into Sudan, meets White Nile to form Nile at Khartoum.
- Origin: lake Tana of Ethiopia.
- Flows across Ethiopia and Sudan.

Great Ruaha River
- Drying up due to climate change and mismanagement.
- One of Tanzania's major rivers.
- Important tributary of Rufiji River (largest river basin in Tanzania).
- Main water source for the Ruaha National Park.

Seine River
- Paris opened the Seine River to public swimming for first time since 1923.
- Second longest river in France after Loire.
- Flows through Paris, emptying into the English Channel at Le Havre.
